nativeRange.habitat: The Singular Bannerfish inhabits deep lagoons and seaward reefs characterized by rich coral growth and high vertical relief. Juveniles tend to be secretive and are more commonly found on inshore reefs, while adults are typically solitary or found in small groups, usually at depths of 15 to 40 m, though the species has been recorded across a broader range from 2 to 250 m.

diet.notes: Generally accepts frozen, freeze-dried, fresh, and flake foods in captivity. In the wild it has been documented feeding on coral polyps, benthic invertebrates, and algae. A varied diet including meaty items such as mysis and finely chopped seafood alongside marine algae and vitamin-enriched preparations is commonly advised. Newly imported individuals can be initially shy feeders and tend to benefit from live or frozen enticement foods to begin feeding.

avoidWith: other Heniochus species unless introduced simultaneously

summary: The largest member of the bannerfish genus, reaching around 30 cm with bold black bands and canary-yellow accents. Generally considered peaceful and is typically housed in FOWLR systems; not regarded as reef safe owing to a documented appetite for coral polyps and tubeworms.
careNotes:
The Singular Bannerfish is the largest member of the Heniochus group and an active swimmer, so a long-footprint tank with ample open water and complex rockwork is generally advised. Adults reach roughly 30 cm, and the species benefits from rich vertical relief and open swimming space — a noticeably larger tank than the minimum is typically recommended for full-grown individuals. Specimens can be housed singly or in small groups when introduced simultaneously; pairs and small groups tend to be more stable than a solo adult added later to an established system. New arrivals are commonly shy and may require extra acclimation time before they begin feeding reliably.

Reef compatibility: Like most Heniochus species, the Singular Bannerfish feeds on coral polyps in the wild and has been reported to nip LPS and SPS corals (large- and small-polyp stony corals), zoanthids, tubeworms, and the mantles of tridacnid clams. It is generally not considered reef safe and is best suited to FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) or fish-dominant systems.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria

additional.name: Wikipedia (Heniochus singularius)

Used for husbandry-adjacent corroboration only.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; forms pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding is not meaningfully documented in home aquariums.
